topic: Type System - repr(transparent) for Newtype FFI
content: When we need to wrap a primitive value like an integer or float in a new type without changing its binary size, the #[repr(transparent)] attribute is your most reliable tool. Imagine you are building a safe payment system where you must expose a u16 amount directly to a C function that expects a pointer to u16. If you simply define a struct with a single field, say 'pub struct Amount { inner: u16 }', the compiler will apply default alignment rules and likely pad your struct to 2 bytes or more depending on the architecture, breaking the ABI compatibility required for the C side. In contrast, #[repr(transparent)] explicitly guarantees that the layout of this new type is identical to its inner field, effectively making it a zero-cost wrapper in terms of memory layout. This distinction becomes critical when comparing #[repr(transparent)] against #[repr(C)], because while both ensure predictable memory layouts suitable for FFI, #[repr(C) on a single-field struct still incurs potential padding overhead unless the field itself is already packed to exactly one byte, whereas transparent removes all ambiguity by inheriting the exact size and alignment of the contained value. The practical application here often involves wrapping pointers as well; consider using #[repr(transparent)] with NonNull<T> instead of Box or Rc. If you wrap a heap-allocated pointer in a Box, the allocation metadata is lost upon drop and memory safety becomes fragile for FFI boundaries. By creating an opaque newtype like 'pub struct SafePtr { inner: NonNull<u8> }' and annotating it with #[repr(transparent)]', you ensure that a pointer to this struct is indistinguishable from a raw pointer to the underlying data in binary size, allowing safe casting without runtime allocation overhead. A common pitfall developers encounter is assuming that any single-field struct automatically satisfies FFI requirements; if you forget #[repr(C)] or #[repr(transparent)], the compiler defaults to standard alignment which may add padding bytes that invalidate the C function signature. Another subtle misconception arises when trying to combine transparency with enum variants; remember that repr(transparent) only applies if the enum has a single variant and that variant is itself transparent, otherwise multiple variants imply different sizes regardless of their internal representation. In real-world scenarios like Rust bindings for Python or Go, this pattern allows you to define your domain-specific types in Rust while letting them interact seamlessly with external C libraries without needing manual size calculations or custom FFI glue code.
